14	# Blueprint Clean Subagent
15	
16	Your job is to read and refine a blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) to ensure it meets strict quality, purity, and citation standards.
17	
18	## Your Tasks
19	
20	1. **Strip Lean Leakage & Project History:**
21	   - Remove any mention of Lean tactics, typeclass notes, or specific Lean implementation strategies.
22	   - Remove project history references like "since iteration N", "our failed route", etc.
23	   - Ensure the blueprint reads as a timeless, standalone mathematical document.
24	2. **Trim Verbosity:**
25	   - Remove useless, overly conversational paragraphs.
26	   - Remove passages that are irrelevant or redundantly discuss things already implemented, unless they serve a mathematical purpose.
27	   - Keep the prose concise and mathematically precise.
28	3. **Enforce Citation Discipline & Add Quotes:**
29	   - Verify every block derived from a reference has a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`.
30	   - **If the quote is missing** but the reference exists in `references/`, you MUST open the actual reference `.pdf` or `.tex` file (using the `.md` index file as a pointer to find the section/page), extract the verbatim quote, and add it to the blueprint.
31	   - **If a reference SHOULD be cited** but isn't mentioned by the writer, and the source is missing from `references/`, spawn a `reference-retriever` to fetch it.
32	4. **Fix LaTeX:**
33	   - Correct basic LaTeX syntax errors.
34	   - Ensure `\uses{}` and `\label{}` cross-references are formatted correctly.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).

41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}

68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}

80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}

86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.

92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}

95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,

101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)

128	\begin{definition}[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
129	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
130	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
131	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
132	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition

136	  % L5594--5617).
137	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
138	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
139	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
140	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
141	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
142	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
143	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
144	  % between abelian categories and assume
145	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
146	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
147	  % \begin{enumerate}
148	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
149	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
150	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
151	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.

153	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
154	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
155	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
156	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
157	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
158	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
159	  \[
160	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
161	  \]

163	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
164	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
165	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
166	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
167	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
168	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
169	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.

171	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
172	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
173	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
174	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
175	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
176	\end{definition}

178	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
179	
180	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
181	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
182	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
183	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
184	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
185	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
186	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
187	lift.
188	
189	\begin{lemma}[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
190	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
191	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
192	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
193	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
194	  \[
195	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
196	  \]
197	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
198	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
199	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
200	  \[
201	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
202	  \]
203	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
204	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
205	  \[
206	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
207	      \longrightarrow 0
208	  \]
209	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
210	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
211	\end{lemma}
212	
213	\begin{proof}
214	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
215	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma; see e.g.\ Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
216	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8 (stated there for projective resolutions; the
217	  injective-resolution form is the dual). Choose injective resolutions
218	  \(A \to I_A^\bullet\) and \(C \to I_C^\bullet\), which exist because \(\mathcal{A}\)
219	  has enough injectives. Set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) in each degree; this is
220	  injective, being a finite direct sum of injectives. The maps
221	  \(I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and \(I_B^n \to I_C^n\) are the canonical coproduct inclusion
222	  and product projection, so each degree is split exact by construction. It remains
223	  to build the augmentation \(B \to I_B^0\) and the differentials
224	  \(I_B^n \to I_B^{n+1}\) making \(I_B^\bullet\) a resolution of \(B\) and the two
225	  vertical families chain maps. One proceeds degree by degree: at each stage the
226	  required lift exists because the relevant target summand \(I_A^{\bullet}\) is
227	  injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism, so the universal lifting
228	  property of injectives produces the missing component; the diagonal differential of
229	  \(I_B^\bullet\) is then assembled from the differentials of \(I_A^\bullet\),
230	  \(I_C^\bullet\) and these lifts. Exactness of the resulting \(I_B^\bullet\) (that it
231	  resolves \(B\)) follows from the long exact homology sequence
232	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to the degreewise-split short
233	  exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet \to 0\): the
234	  outer complexes are acyclic resolutions, so the connecting maps force
235	  \(I_B^\bullet\) to be acyclic in positive degrees and to have \(H^0 = B\).
236	\end{proof}

238	\section{The dimension-shift step}
239	
240	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
241	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
242	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
243	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
244	complex-level homology long exact sequence
245	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
246	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
247	sub-lemma below.
248	
249	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
250	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
251	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
252	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
253	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
254	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
255	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
256	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
257	  % between abelian categories and assume
258	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
259	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
260	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
261	  % \begin{enumerate}
262	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
263	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
264	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
265	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
266	  % \end{enumerate}
267	  % In each of the three cases
268	  % $$
269	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
270	  % $$
271	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
272	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
273	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
274	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
275	  \[
276	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
277	  \]
278	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
279	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
280	  \begin{enumerate}
281	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
282	      furnish isomorphisms
283	      \[
284	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
285	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
286	      \]
287	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
288	      \[
289	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
290	      \]
291	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
292	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
293	  \end{enumerate}
294	\end{lemma}
295	
296	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
297	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
298	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
299	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
300	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
301	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
302	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
303	\begin{proof}
304	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
305	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
306	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
307	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
308	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
309	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
310	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
311	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
312	
313	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
314	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
315	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
316	  complexes
317	  \[
318	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
319	  \]
320	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
321	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
322	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
323	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
324	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
325	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
326	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
327	  \[
328	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
329	      \longrightarrow 0
330	  \]
331	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
332	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
333	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
334	  \[
335	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
336	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
337	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
338	  \]
339	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
340	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
341	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
342	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
343	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
344	  sequence of right-derived functors
345	  \[
346	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
347	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
348	  \]
349	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
350	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
351	  \[
352	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
353	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
354	  \]
355	
356	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
357	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
358	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
359	  \[
360	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
361	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
362	  \]
363	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
364	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
365	  In the lowest degree the segment
366	  \[
367	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
368	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
369	  \]
370	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
371	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
372	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
373	  part (2).
374	\end{proof}

449	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
450	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
451	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
452	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, with the long exact sequence at each
453	  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
454	  resolution into the
455	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
456	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
457	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
458	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
459	  \[
460	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
461	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
462	  \]
463	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
464	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.
465	
466	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
467	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
468	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
469	  \[
470	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
471	      = (R^0 G)(A),
472	  \]
473	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
474	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
475	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
476	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
477	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
478	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
479	  \[
480	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
481	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
482	  \]
483	  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
484	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
485	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
486	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
487	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
488	
489	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
490	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
491	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
492	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
493	  \[
494	    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
495	  \]
496	  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
497	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
498	  isomorphisms down the staircase
499	  \[
500	    (R^n G)(A)
501	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
502	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
503	      \;\cong\; \cdots
504	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
505	  \]
506	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
508	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
509	  \[
510	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
511	  \]
512	  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
513	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
514	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
515	  \[
516	    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
517	      \;\cong\;
518	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
519	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
520	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
521	  \]
522	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
523	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
524	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
525	
526	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel
527	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
528	  horseshoe lift (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) and the complex-level
529	  homology long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) --- on
530	  the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\)
531	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). The injective objects supplied
532	  by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
533	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
534	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
535	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
536	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
537	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
10	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).

12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
20	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
21	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
22	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
23	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
24	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
25	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
26	
27	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
28	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
29	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
30	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
31	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
32	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
33	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
34	\[
35	  \begin{array}{ccc}
36	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
37	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
38	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
39	  \end{array}
40	\]
41	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
42	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
43	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
44	
45	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
46	
47	\begin{definition}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
51	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
53	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
54	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
55	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
56	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
57	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
58	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
59	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
60	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
61	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
62	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
63	  % the unit ideal, see
64	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
65	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
66	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
67	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
68	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
69	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
70	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
71	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
72	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
73	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
74	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
75	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
76	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
77	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
78	  \[
79	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
80	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
81	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
82	  \]
83	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
84	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
85	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
86	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
87	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
88	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
89	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
90	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
91	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
92	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
93	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
94	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
95	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
96	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
97	\end{definition}

100

103	  \label{def:cech_complex}
104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
105	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
106	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
107	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
108	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
109	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
110	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$

115	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to

117	  % $$

122	  % M \to

132	  vanishing).}
133	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
134	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
135	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
136	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
137	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
138	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
139	  in degree \(p\) it is

142	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
143	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
144	  \]
145	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the

147	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
148	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
149	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
150	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex

152	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
153	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
154	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
155	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
156	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*

158	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
159	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated

161	\end{definition}

163	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
164	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
165	
166	It is convenient to factor the construction of
167	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
168	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the entire mathematical
169	content that is not yet available off the shelf is isolated in the middle piece.
170	
171	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
172	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
173	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
174	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
175	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
176	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
177	\[
178	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
179	    \;=\;
180	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
181	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
182	\]
183	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
184	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
185	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
186	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
187	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
188	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
189	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
190	
191	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
192	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
193	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
194	\[
195	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
196	  \qquad
197	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
198	\]
199	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
200	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
201	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
202	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
203	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
204	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
205	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
206	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
207	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
208	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
209	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
210	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
211	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
212	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
213	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
214	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. This
215	functoriality is \emph{not} coupled to the project's tensor--pullback substrate;
216	it consumes only Mathlib's pseudofunctor packaging of pullback and pushforward.
217	Concretely, pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
218	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
219	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
220	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
222	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts about this
223	packaging: the mate identity expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback
224	comparison as the pushforward comparison (the lemma
225	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}), its identity normalisation
226	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}, and the pseudofunctor unitor and pentagon
227	(associativity) coherences \texttt{pseudofunctor\_left\_unitality},
228	\texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}.
229	All of these are already available off the shelf, so the functoriality of the
230	relative-direct-image functor is a \emph{consumer of Mathlib's coherence},
231	independent of the project-local sheafification/pullback machinery of the
232	tensor--pullback substrate. The laws are nonetheless not formal trivialities: each
233	is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison isomorphisms along
234	the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and past the adjunction
235	unit, gluing them across two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions; the obstacle is the
236	bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pushforward/pullback pseudofunctor coherence supplied by
254	Mathlib (the comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities), not any project-local ingredient.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275

284	  \[

286	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}

288	  \]

290	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
291	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).

299	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns

306	\begin{definition}\leanok
307	[Push--pull comparison map]
308	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
310	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
311	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
312	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
313	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
314	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
315	  \[
316	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
318	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
320	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \;=\;
322	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
323	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
324	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
325	  \]
326	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
327	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
328	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
329	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
330	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
331	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
332	\end{definition}

334	\begin{lemma}
335	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
336	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
338	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
339	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
340	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
341	\end{lemma}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	  A statement purely about the pushforward/pullback comparison isomorphisms, with no
345	  sectionwise content. Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward
346	  comparison as the conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison
347	  (\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}); the identity leg then collapses by the
348	  identity normalisation \texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom} together with the right
349	  unitality \texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, and the two over-triangle
350	  \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
351	\end{proof}

353	\begin{lemma}
354	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
355	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
357	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
358	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
359	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
360	  \[
361	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
362	  \]
363	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
364	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
365	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
366	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
367	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
368	\end{lemma}
369	
370	\begin{proof}
371	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
372	  The composition law is proved by reducing the push--pull comparison map to its
373	  transport-free \emph{raw} form, in which the two structure maps \(p_1, p_2\) and the
374	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\) enter as free data rather than being baked into the
375	  over-category morphism. This bridge between the comparison map of
376	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} and its raw form holds definitionally, so it carries no
377	  content; its purpose is to expose the two over-triangle \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions, which
378	  are then eliminated by substituting the defining equalities of the structure maps — making
379	  the transports trivial. On the raw form the identity decomposes into three ingredients.
380	  First, the composite adjunction unit splits: the unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) of the composite is
381	  expressed through the iterated units \(\eta^{g}, \eta^{h}\) by the mate core
382	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and its solved form, together with the naturality of the
383	  inner pullback--pushforward adjunction unit. Second, the resulting pullback-side identity is
384	  the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell
385	  relating the two bracketings of the comparison isomorphism
386	  \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\) — wrapped as the pentagon brick. Third, the
387	  over-triangle transport coherences remaining after the substitutions are discharged by
388	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}. Assembling the three gives
389	  \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\).
390	\end{proof}

392	\begin{lemma}
393	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
394	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
395	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
396	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
397	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
398	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
399	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
400	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
401	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
402	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
403	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
404	  \[
405	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
406	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
407	    \;=\;
408	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
409	  \]
410	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
411	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
412	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
413	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
414	\end{lemma}

416	\begin{proof}
417	  Proved directly in Lean.
418	\end{proof}

420	\begin{lemma}
421	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
422	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
424	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
425	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
426	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
427	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
428	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
429	  collapses to the transport-light form
430	  \[
431	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
432	      \;\circ\; e_2
433	    \;=\;
434	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
435	      \;\circ\; e_3,
436	  \]
437	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
438	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
439	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
440	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
441	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
442	  composition (pentagon) law.
443	\end{lemma}

446	  Proved directly in Lean.
447	\end{proof}

725	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
726	
727	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
728	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
729	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
730	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
731	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
732	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.

796	  % \texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} (the higher direct images are the
797	  % sheafifications of the cohomology presheaves of preimages) (read from
798	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
799	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
800	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
801	  % The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
802	  % associated to the presheaves
803	  % $$
804	  % V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
805	  % $$
806	  % with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
807	  % There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
808	  % bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$."
809	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
810	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
811	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
812	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For every \(k \geq 0\) the higher direct image
813	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{G})\) is the sheaf
814	  on \(S\) associated to the presheaf
815	  \[
816	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
817	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
818	  \]
819	  with the evident restriction maps. In particular \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) vanishes
820	  iff \(H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for every \(V\) in a basis of
821	  the topology of \(S\); since the affine opens form such a basis, it suffices to
822	  check the vanishing on affine \(V\). This is the local-on-the-base description
823	  through which \(f_*\)-acyclicity is reduced to absolute cohomology vanishing on
824	  preimages.

826	
827	\begin{proof}
828	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
829	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
830	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
831	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\); since the abelian
832	  structure on \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules computes cokernels and kernels objectwise on
833	  the level of presheaves and then sheafifies, that cohomology sheaf is the
834	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
835	  = H^k\bigl(\mathcal{I}^\bullet(f^{-1}(V))\bigr) = H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\),
836	  the last equality because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is an injective (hence
837	  acyclic) resolution computing the absolute cohomology of \(f^{-1}(V)\). Evaluation
838	  of a sheafification vanishes exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis,
839	  giving the stated affine-local criterion.
840	\end{proof}

850	  % L180--199) and Stacks Project, Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-relative-Leray}
851	  % (the relative Leray / Grothendieck composition spectral sequence) (read from

853	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.

859	  % $$
860	  % E_2^{p, q} = R^pg_*(R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
861	  % $$
862	  % converging to $R^{p + q}(g \circ f)_*\mathcal{F}$.

864	  % is a version for bounded below complexes of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."

866	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}, and Cohomology,
867	  \texttt{lemma-relative-Leray}.}
868	  Let \(X\) be separated, \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open immersion of an affine
869	  open \(U\), and \(f : X \to S\) any morphism, with composite \(g := f \circ j : U
870	  \to S\). Then:
871	  \begin{enumerate}
872	    \item \(j\) is an affine morphism (an open immersion of an affine open into a
873	      separated scheme), so its higher direct images vanish: \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} = 0\)
874	      for all \(q \geq 1\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
875	      \(\mathcal{H}\); and
876	    \item consequently, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{H}\) on \(U\) and every
877	      \(k \geq 0\),
878	      \[
879	        R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \;\cong\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H}.
880	      \]
881	  \end{enumerate}
882	\end{lemma}
883	
884	\begin{proof}
885	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
886	  (1) An open immersion of an affine open \(U\) into a separated scheme \(X\) is an
887	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
888	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
889	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
890	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
891	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
892	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
893	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
894	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
895	
896	  (2) Apply the relative Leray spectral sequence to the composition \(g = f \circ j\):
897	  it has second page \(E_2^{p, q} = R^p f_*\bigl(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr)\) and
898	  converges to \(R^{p+q} g_*\mathcal{H}\). By part (1) the rows with \(q \geq 1\)
899	  vanish, so the spectral sequence is concentrated on the single row \(q = 0\) and
900	  degenerates; its edge maps give the isomorphism \(R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) =
901	  R^k f_*\bigl(R^0 j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \cong R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\) for every \(k\).
902	\end{proof}
